topic: [agriculture_food/industry_problem] Our fresh produce packing facility is struggling to reduce post-harvest losses from bruising and microbial spoilage during washing, grading, and cold storage. W
full_prompt: Our fresh produce packing facility is struggling to reduce post-harvest losses from bruising and microbial spoilage during washing, grading, and cold storage. What process changes or monitoring technologies could help us identify the main loss points and extend shelf life without slowing throughput?
completion:
I’ll first identify recent literature that connects packinghouse loss mechanisms (mechanical bruising, wash-water contamination, cold-chain breakdown) with monitoring technologies that do not materially slow line speed. Then I’ll read a couple of representative papers and translate the evidence into a practical facility-level intervention genome.  

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"fresh produce packinghouse postharvest losses bruising mechanical damage monitoring machine vision cold storage shelf life 2018 2025","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```  

[result]: The search returned several relevant postharvest-engineering papers, including reviews on non-destructive quality sensing and mechanical-damage detection in fruits and vegetables. Strong candidates included OpenAlex works `https://openalex.org/W3179479086` on imaging/spectroscopy for postharvest quality monitoring, `https://openalex.org/W3039022237` on non-destructive detection of bruising/defects, and `https://openalex.org/W4388126457` on digital technologies for reducing postharvest losses. The highest-value thread for this facility problem appears to be combining a short “loss-point audit” with in-line optical sensing and targeted handling changes.  

To ground the mechanical-loss side, I’ll read a recent review/cross-cutting paper on non-destructive quality and defect monitoring so we can distinguish feasible in-line tools from laboratory-only methods.  

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"https://openalex.org/W3179479086"}
```  

[result]: This paper summarizes optical and sensor-based postharvest quality monitoring methods, especially RGB/machine vision, hyperspectral or multispectral imaging, near-infrared spectroscopy, and thermal/fluorescence approaches. It emphasizes that RGB vision is easiest to deploy at commercial throughput for size, color, visible defects, and sorting consistency, while hyperspectral/multispectral methods can detect subtler bruising, water-soaked tissue, decay risk, or internal-quality proxies but require model calibration by commodity and cultivar. For a packing facility, the actionable takeaway is to start with low-friction monitoring—line cameras plus periodic destructive/near-destructive validation—then add spectral inspection at high-loss transfer points or before cold storage if the economics justify it.  

Next I’ll search specifically for microbial spoilage and wash-water management, because extending shelf life without reducing throughput usually depends as much on water quality, cross-contamination control, and rapid cooling as on grading accuracy.  

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"fresh produce wash water sanitizer cross contamination microbial spoilage packinghouse cold storage shelf life monitoring ORP turbidity 2018 2025","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```  

[result]: The search found recent food-safety and postharvest papers on sanitizer efficacy, wash-water organic load, cross-contamination dynamics, and cold-chain control. Relevant OpenAlex candidates included `https://openalex.org/W3039783424` on wash-water disinfection and cross-contamination risk, `https://openalex.org/W3201242102` on fresh-produce decontamination technologies, and `https://openalex.org/W4296209841` on cold-chain monitoring and shelf-life prediction. The recurring finding is that poor real-time control of free sanitizer, pH, oxidation-reduction potential, turbidity/organic load, and water temperature can turn washing from a cleaning step into a contamination-spreading step.  

I’ll read the wash-water-focused paper to extract the operational controls most likely to reduce microbial spoilage without adding a slow manual inspection step.  

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"https://openalex.org/W3039783424"}
```  

[result]: The paper highlights that microbial risk during produce washing is governed by the balance between sanitizer residual, organic load, pH, temperature, contact time, and produce surface characteristics. It stresses that sanitizer concentration alone is insufficient: high organic matter can rapidly consume free chlorine or other oxidants, while unstable pH and dirty recirculated water increase cross-contamination risk. For a high-throughput facility, the paper supports automated dosing and continuous logging of free chlorine or peracetic acid, pH, ORP, turbidity, flow rate, and water temperature, paired with alarms and dump/refill triggers rather than slower batch testing alone.  

Before proposing, I’ll check whether the combined mechanism—instrumented loss-point audit plus in-line bruise/defect sensing and wash/cold-chain control—is already a standard packaged intervention or whether the novelty lies in integrating it for root-cause attribution.  

```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","mechanism":"Fresh produce packinghouse loss-point attribution system combining instrumented dummy produce for impact mapping, in-line RGB or multispectral defect detection, continuous wash-water sanitizer/ORP/pH/turbidity monitoring, and cold-room temperature-humidity logging to prioritize process changes without slowing throughput","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025}
```  

[result]: The component technologies are well represented individually: instrumented spheres or dummy fruits for impact assessment, optical inspection for defects, wash-water monitoring for food safety, and cold-chain data loggers for shelf-life control. Fewer papers describe these as one integrated loss-attribution workflow that links bruising, microbial spoilage, and storage abuse to specific packinghouse unit operations. The most defensible contribution is therefore not a new sensor, but a practical “loss map + intervention loop” that ranks main loss points by economic loss per minute of throughput and verifies changes through shelf-life outcomes.  

Based on the evidence, I’ll propose an industry-oriented intervention that starts with diagnosis, then deploys only the monitoring and process changes likely to preserve line speed.  

```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Deploy a two-stage packinghouse loss-reduction program: first, a 2- to 4-week loss-point audit using instrumented dummy produce/impact loggers, synchronized line-speed and transfer-point video, in-line RGB defect counts, wash-water sensor logs, and cold-room temperature/humidity records; second, targeted process changes at the highest-loss points, such as lowering drop heights, adding cushioned transitions, synchronizing belt speeds, reducing bin-to-flume impact, improving brush/roller settings, automating sanitizer dosing, setting turbidity or organic-load dump/refill triggers, and tightening precooling/cold-storage setpoints. The monitoring layer should timestamp each lot through washing, grading, packing, and storage so later bruise incidence, decay incidence, weight loss, and customer rejection can be traced back to specific equipment zones and environmental excursions.","niche_genome":"Fresh produce packing facilities handling bruise-sensitive and decay-prone commodities such as apples, pears, peaches, tomatoes, berries, leafy greens, cucumbers, peppers, and fresh-cut produce. Best suited to operations that already run high throughput and cannot solve losses by slowing the line, adding extensive manual inspection, or relying only on end-of-line quality checks. The approach fits facilities with recurring shrink, inconsistent shelf life, high customer complaints, or uncertainty over whether losses originate in receiving, washing, grading, packing, or cold storage.","observation_genome":"Recent postharvest literature supports several linked observations: mechanical damage often occurs at discrete transfer points, drops, rollers, or bin-handling steps and may only become visible later as bruising or decay; optical imaging can quantify visible defects at line speed and, with multispectral or hyperspectral calibration, can detect some less-visible tissue damage or decay risk; wash systems can reduce surface contamination but can also spread microbes if sanitizer residual, pH, organic load, turbidity, and temperature are poorly controlled; and small cold-chain deviations or high humidity/condensation events can accelerate microbial growth and reduce shelf life. Therefore, the facility needs time-linked root-cause attribution, not just more final inspection.","limitation_genome":"The proposal does not eliminate the need for commodity-specific validation. Bruise thresholds, spectral signatures, sanitizer tolerance, water-contact sensitivity, and ideal humidity/temperature ranges vary by crop, cultivar, maturity, and packaging format. Low-cost RGB systems may miss subsurface bruising, while hyperspectral systems can be expensive and require calibration maintenance. Wash-water sensors can drift or foul and must be verified with routine microbiological and chemical testing. Some process fixes, such as reducing drop height or changing transfer geometry, may require capital modifications or planned downtime. The program should therefore begin with a focused pilot on the top 1-2 commodities or lines with the highest economic loss.","delta_genome":"Compared with a conventional approach of periodic manual quality checks, occasional sanitizer tests, and reactive customer-complaint investigations, this system creates a continuous loss map across the packing process. The key delta is linking four data streams—impact events, visible/subvisible defects, wash-water control, and cold-storage conditions—to lot-level shelf-life outcomes. This allows managers to prioritize changes by measured loss contribution: for example, a specific elevator drop causing bruise-related shrink, a wash tank losing sanitizer during high-soil lots, a grading brush causing skin breaks, or a cold-room door cycle causing condensation and decay. Throughput is protected because monitoring occurs in-line or through short diagnostic audits rather than slower manual sorting.","claim_genome":"A fresh produce facility can reduce post-harvest losses without slowing throughput by implementing an integrated loss-point attribution program: instrument the line to identify damaging impacts, use in-line vision or spectral inspection to quantify defect trends, continuously control wash-water chemistry and organic load, and log cold-storage conditions by lot. The recommended first actions are: run instrumented dummy produce through each line and commodity format; install or activate continuous wash-water monitoring for sanitizer residual, pH, ORP, turbidity, temperature, and flow; add camera-based defect dashboards at grading and post-pack checkpoints; tighten precooling and cold-room alarm limits; and review weekly lot-level shelf-life data to rank interventions by shrink reduction per dollar. Expected benefits are fewer bruises, lower cross-contamination risk, more consistent shelf life, faster root-cause investigations, and better evidence for equipment or sanitation investments."}}
```

topic: [urban_planning/policy_question] What studies should we commission to understand how vacant commercial corridors can be converted into mixed-use community assets while supporting small business
full_prompt: What studies should we commission to understand how vacant commercial corridors can be converted into mixed-use community assets while supporting small businesses and avoiding speculative displacement?
completion:
I’ll first map the recent literature around vacant corridors, greyfield retrofit, land banking, and anti-displacement so the commissioned-study agenda is grounded in both reuse mechanisms and land/control questions.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"vacant commercial corridors mixed-use adaptive reuse small business anti-displacement land banking greyfields urban regeneration","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```

[result]: The search surfaces a useful split between precinct-scale “greyfield” regeneration literature and institutional land-control literature. The strongest matches among the provided candidates are **oa:W3217183307, _Greening the Greyfields_** for retrofit/regeneration models and **oa:W2969974915, _Banking on land_** for land banking as a governance tool. Other candidates are less directly aligned with commercial-corridor reuse or displacement prevention.

Next I’ll read the greyfield-regeneration source to understand what it contributes about converting underused commercial/auto-oriented sites into mixed-use precincts.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W3217183307"}
```

[result]: _Greening the Greyfields_ frames underused urban land as a precinct-scale opportunity rather than a parcel-by-parcel redevelopment problem. It emphasizes coordinated governance, infrastructure retrofits, sustainability upgrades, and mixed-use design as central to transforming obsolete commercial or low-intensity areas. For this policy question, its key contribution is the idea that vacant commercial corridors should be studied as networked precincts with land assembly, mobility, public realm, and housing/business ecosystems considered together.

Now I’ll read the land-banking review to assess tools for preventing speculative acquisition and aligning reuse with community benefit.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W2969974915"}
```

[result]: _Banking on land_ reviews how land banks have evolved from vacancy-management tools into instruments of uneven urban redevelopment. It warns that land banking can support public-interest reuse, but can also reproduce speculative growth logics if not paired with accountability, affordability requirements, and community governance. For vacant commercial corridors, the study suggests that ownership/control mechanisms are as important as design proposals: who acquires land, under what mandates, and with what resale or leasing restrictions will shape displacement outcomes.

To avoid commissioning duplicative work, I’ll check whether the specific mechanism—commercial-corridor conversion combined with small-business retention and anti-speculation land governance—appears as a well-developed recent research package.

```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","mechanism":"precinct-scale conversion of vacant commercial corridors into mixed-use community assets using public/community land control, small-business retention tools, and anti-speculative displacement safeguards","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025}
```

[result]: Recent work covers adjacent components—greyfield retrofit, land banking, commercial vacancy recovery, community land trusts, and anti-displacement policy—but these are often studied separately. There appears to be a gap in integrated empirical research linking corridor-scale reuse feasibility, small-business operating conditions, land-control instruments, and speculative-displacement indicators. A commissioned study portfolio could therefore be novel if it tests how these mechanisms interact in specific corridor typologies rather than treating reuse as only a design or real-estate problem.

Based on the literature, the key research gap is not simply “what can vacant corridors become,” but “which conversion pathways create community assets without triggering rent escalation, business churn, or land speculation.” I’ll now propose a commissioned research agenda organized around mechanism, niche, observations, limitations, deltas, and claims.

```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Commission an integrated, mixed-method study portfolio on corridor conversion that combines: 1) parcel/building reuse feasibility analysis; 2) small-business ecosystem diagnostics; 3) public/community land-control scenarios such as land banks, community land trusts, public development authorities, limited-equity ownership, and master leasing; 4) anti-displacement policy modeling including commercial rent stabilization proxies, right-to-return provisions, community benefit agreements, below-market commercial space, tax abatements tied to local ownership, and anti-flipping covenants; and 5) participatory design of mixed-use community assets such as affordable housing over retail, childcare, health clinics, cultural space, maker space, fresh-food retail, civic plazas, and social-service hubs.","niche_genome":"Focus on vacant or underperforming commercial corridors, especially older strip-retail corridors, greyfield shopping centers, obsolete office/retail clusters, and auto-oriented arterials in neighborhoods facing either disinvestment or early speculative pressure. Prioritize corridors with high small-business dependence, immigrant or minority-owned business presence, transit access or future transit investment, and fragmented private ownership. The study should be designed for municipal planning departments, redevelopment agencies, community development corporations, land banks, small-business offices, and philanthropic or public mission-driven investors.","observation_genome":"Collect parcel ownership, vacancy, code condition, sales, lease, tax delinquency, eviction, business-license, and rent data to identify speculation and displacement risk. Conduct merchant surveys and interviews on lease terms, operating margins, customer base, relocation risk, capital needs, and desired public investments. Map community asset gaps such as food access, health care, childcare, public space, cultural facilities, and affordable housing. Use scenario modeling to compare redevelopment pathways: market-rate mixed use, public land banking, community ownership, phased rehabilitation, cooperative commercial ownership, and public-private partnership models. Include before/after or matched-corridor case studies from places that have attempted greyfield regeneration, land banking, or commercial corridor revitalization.","limitation_genome":"Existing literature often treats corridor revitalization as either a physical design problem or an economic development problem, with insufficient integration of land governance, small-business survival, and displacement prevention. Data on commercial rents, informal leases, landlord behavior, and beneficial ownership are often incomplete. Land banks and public acquisition tools can help but may also accelerate speculative redevelopment if mandates and disposition rules are weak. Community engagement can be extractive unless residents and merchants have decision rights, compensation, and long-term governance roles. Findings will be context-sensitive because state enabling laws, tax systems, market strength, and local institutional capacity vary widely.","delta_genome":"The commissioned work should move beyond a generic corridor plan by producing: 1) a typology of vacant commercial corridors by market condition, ownership fragmentation, building adaptability, and displacement risk; 2) an anti-speculation early-warning dashboard using property sales, LLC ownership changes, tax appeals, rent increases, vacancies, and permit activity; 3) a small-business retention model estimating which firms can survive construction, rent changes, and customer disruption; 4) a land-control decision tree showing when to use land banking, CLTs, public acquisition, master leasing, zoning overlays, or community benefit agreements; 5) pro formas comparing community-serving reuse with conventional speculative redevelopment; and 6) implementation pilots with measurable community-benefit metrics.","claim_genome":"Cities should commission a portfolio of studies rather than a single market-feasibility report. The core claim to test is that vacant commercial corridors can become mixed-use community assets only when physical reuse, small-business retention, and anti-speculative land governance are planned together from the start. The most policy-relevant outputs would be a corridor typology, displacement-risk dashboard, small-business stabilization toolkit, land-control strategy, and pilot implementation plan. Success should be measured not only by reduced vacancy or increased assessed value, but by retention of local businesses, creation of affordable commercial and residential space, community ownership/control, improved access to services, and prevention of involuntary residential or commercial displacement."}}
```

[result]: Proposed commissioned research agenda: a coordinated set of empirical, policy, and design studies focused on converting vacant commercial corridors into mixed-use community assets while protecting existing small businesses and limiting speculative displacement. The agenda draws from greyfield-regeneration literature and land-banking critiques, but extends them into a practical policy framework centered on corridor typologies, land-control mechanisms, business retention, and measurable anti-displacement outcomes.

topic: [cognitive_science/academic_question] What role does predictive processing play in perceptual adaptation under uncertainty, and how might this framework explain differences between typical cognition

completion:
I first scope the 2018–2025 literature around predictive processing, perceptual adaptation, uncertainty, and neurodivergence, with emphasis on autism because it is the most developed predictive-processing case in current cognitive science.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "predictive processing perceptual adaptation uncertainty autism neurodivergent perception precision weighting", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```

[result]: The search suggests three recurring clusters: hierarchical Bayesian / predictive-coding accounts of perception; precision-weighting accounts of uncertainty and prediction error; and neurodivergence-focused work, especially autism, linking sensory atypicalities to altered priors, prediction errors, or contextual modulation. The strongest conceptual bridge is that perceptual adaptation depends not only on sensory input but on how the system estimates uncertainty and assigns precision to priors versus incoming evidence.

The most directly relevant candidate is the 2024 review on auditory processing and the autistic profile, since auditory perception is a common domain for studying sensory prediction, adaptation, habituation, and uncertainty.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4404473664"}
```

[result]: The review frames autistic auditory perception as involving differences in how sensory information is integrated, filtered, and contextualized. It highlights atypical responses to sound, altered habituation, sensory overload, and difficulties using context to disambiguate auditory input. For the present question, the paper supports a predictive-processing interpretation: autistic perception may involve different calibration of prediction errors and sensory precision, especially when auditory environments are noisy, ambiguous, or rapidly changing.

To avoid treating neurodivergent perception as a simple deficit model, I next read the developmental-diversity paper, which appears useful for framing differences as dynamic, developmental, and context-sensitive.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4313621133"}
```

[result]: This paper argues that research on neurodevelopmental conditions should move beyond static biological categories and reintroduce developmental, contextual, and diversity-sensitive explanations. It cautions against explaining autism, ADHD, and other neurodevelopmental profiles only as “brain differences” detached from environment, task demands, learning history, or social context. This is important for the predictive-processing question because precision-weighting and adaptation are not fixed traits: they may develop through repeated interactions between nervous systems and uncertain environments.

I then check whether the core mechanism—uncertainty-sensitive predictive processing as an explanation of typical versus neurodivergent perceptual adaptation—is already saturated or whether there is room for a sharper contribution.

```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "Perceptual adaptation under uncertainty depends on hierarchical predictive processing in which typical and neurodivergent perceivers differ in precision-weighting of priors and sensory prediction errors, producing different adaptation, habituation, and contextual learning profiles.", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```

[result]: The mechanism is partially established, especially in autism-focused predictive-processing theories, but there is still novelty in specifying perceptual adaptation under uncertainty rather than prediction alone. Existing accounts often debate “weak priors,” “inflexible priors,” or “over-precise sensory prediction errors,” but fewer integrate these into a developmental and task-sensitive account of adaptation across typical and neurodivergent cognition. A promising contribution would distinguish adaptation to stable uncertainty, volatile uncertainty, and socially structured uncertainty.

Given these results, I formulate a research proposal that treats predictive processing as a shared computational framework while avoiding the claim that neurodivergent perception is merely impaired. The central move is to explain differences in adaptation as differences in precision calibration across contexts.

```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Predictive processing explains perceptual adaptation as hierarchical updating of expectations under uncertainty. The brain minimizes prediction error by balancing prior expectations against incoming sensory evidence, but this balance depends on estimated precision: when the world seems reliable, priors guide perception strongly; when uncertainty or volatility is high, sensory evidence and prediction errors receive more weight. Neurodivergent perception, especially in autism and related sensory profiles, may involve different precision calibration across levels of the hierarchy, producing distinctive patterns of adaptation, habituation, sensory overload, and contextual learning.", "niche_genome": "The proposal sits at the intersection of cognitive science, computational psychiatry, neurodiversity studies, and perception research from 2018–2025. It focuses on perceptual adaptation under uncertainty rather than predictive processing in general. The key empirical niche is comparing typical and neurodivergent perceivers in tasks where uncertainty is manipulated explicitly: stable noise, volatile stimulus contingencies, ambiguous multisensory input, and socially contextualized perceptual cues.", "observation_genome": "Typical cognition often shows rapid contextual adaptation: repeated exposure to regularities reduces prediction error, supports habituation, and allows perception to become more efficient. Neurodivergent perception may show different adaptation curves: slower habituation to repeated stimuli, stronger registration of sensory detail, reduced reliance on contextual priors, or difficulty suppressing prediction errors in noisy environments. However, these differences can also confer advantages, such as heightened sensitivity to fine-grained changes, resistance to misleading expectations, and more veridical perception when dominant priors are inaccurate.", "limitation_genome": "Existing predictive-processing accounts sometimes overgeneralize from autism to neurodivergence as a whole and risk pathologizing difference as defective inference. They also disagree on whether autistic perception reflects weak priors, overly precise sensory prediction errors, inflexible priors, or atypical hierarchical coupling. Many studies measure prediction or sensory sensitivity but do not directly manipulate uncertainty, volatility, developmental history, or environmental fit. A further limitation is that neurodivergent groups are heterogeneous, so group-level explanations may obscure individual adaptation strategies.", "delta_genome": "The proposed advance is to treat neurodivergent perceptual adaptation as context-dependent precision calibration rather than as a single global deficit in prediction. It distinguishes three forms of uncertainty: sensory uncertainty, where the signal itself is noisy; environmental volatility, where regularities change over time; and social uncertainty, where cues are ambiguous, norm-dependent, or inconsistently reinforced. This allows the same individual to show reduced adaptation in one context but enhanced perceptual sensitivity or more flexible updating in another. It also aligns predictive-processing theory with neurodiversity-informed accounts that emphasize environment-person fit.", "claim_genome": "Predictive processing plays a central role in perceptual adaptation by determining how perceivers update expectations when sensory evidence is uncertain. Differences between typical and neurodivergent perception can be explained as differences in how precision is assigned to priors, sensory evidence, and prediction errors across hierarchical levels and contexts. Typical cognition may privilege efficient stabilization of perception through context-sensitive priors, whereas neurodivergent cognition may maintain stronger access to sensory prediction errors or calibrate priors differently, especially in uncertain or volatile environments. Therefore, neurodivergent perception should not be understood simply as impaired prediction, but as an alternative adaptive regime whose costs and benefits depend on uncertainty structure, developmental history, and environmental demands."}}
```

[result]: Proposed thesis: predictive processing provides a unifying account of perceptual adaptation under uncertainty by modeling perception as precision-weighted updating. The framework explains typical–neurodivergent differences not as a binary between normal and deficient perception, but as different strategies for managing uncertainty, prediction error, and environmental change.

topic: [cognitive_science/academic_question] What neural and behavioral mechanisms support the development of theory of mind across early childhood, and how are these mechanisms influenced by language expo
full_prompt: What neural and behavioral mechanisms support the development of theory of mind across early childhood, and how are these mechanisms influenced by language exposure and social interaction?
completion:
I’ll start by locating recent literature relevant to theory of mind, mentalizing, language, and social interaction within the requested year window, while checking whether the available candidate set contains usable anchors. The candidate list is broad, so the search step helps establish which papers can serve as mechanistic scaffolds versus which gaps need to be addressed by synthesis.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "theory of mind early childhood language exposure social interaction mentalizing neural mechanisms 2018 2025", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```

[result]: The search returned a mixed set: the most directly relevant provided candidate is oa:W2994905377, “The Mentalizing Approach to Psychopathology,” which summarizes mentalizing dimensions and mechanisms but is not child-specific. oa:W3195973239 on prefrontal cortex and executive function is relevant as a supporting mechanism for false-belief reasoning and self-other inhibition. The remaining candidates are less central to early childhood theory of mind, so the trajectory should synthesize ToM development by linking mentalizing-system accounts with executive-control and language/social-learning mechanisms.
Next I’ll read the mentalizing paper because it likely provides the clearest conceptual bridge between theory of mind, social cognition, and neural systems. I will treat it as a general mechanistic source, then narrow its implications to early childhood development.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W2994905377"}
```

[result]: The paper frames mentalizing as the capacity to interpret behavior in terms of internal mental states and distinguishes multiple dimensions: automatic versus controlled, self- versus other-focused, internal versus external cues, and cognitive versus affective mentalizing. It emphasizes that mentalizing depends on distributed social-cognitive systems rather than a single module, with flexibility needed to shift between perspectives and cue types. For the present question, this suggests that early theory of mind develops through the coordination of representational mental-state concepts, affective attunement, and controlled perspective management.
ToM tasks in preschool years often require children to inhibit their own knowledge and maintain another person’s perspective, so I’ll read the prefrontal-control paper as a second mechanistic anchor. This helps distinguish conceptual mental-state understanding from the executive demands of expressing that understanding in behavior.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W3195973239"}
```

[result]: The paper reviews prefrontal cortex contributions to cognitive control, including maintaining goals, selecting task-relevant information, suppressing habitual responses, and coordinating flexible behavior. It distinguishes controlled from automatic processing and links prefrontal systems to executive functions such as working memory, inhibition, and rule use. Applied to early childhood ToM, these mechanisms help explain why children may show partial or implicit sensitivity to beliefs before they can pass explicit false-belief tasks requiring verbal explanation and response inhibition.
I’ll now extract a structured “genome” from the mentalizing anchor so the final proposal can make explicit what mechanism is inherited and what must be adapted for the early-childhood language/social-interaction question. This is useful because the anchor paper is broad and clinically oriented, whereas the target question is developmental.

```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "oa:W2994905377"}
```

[result]: Extracted mechanism: mentalizing is multidimensional and depends on flexible coordination between automatic and controlled processing, self/other representations, affective and cognitive inference, and internal/external social cues. Extracted niche: social cognition and psychopathology, with emphasis on individual differences in mentalizing capacity and breakdowns under stress or relational difficulty. Extracted limitation: the framework is not primarily designed to explain normative early-childhood developmental change or the specific roles of language exposure, caregiver talk, joint attention, pretend play, and peer interaction.
Before proposing a synthesis, I’ll check novelty around the mechanism I am converging on: language-guided social interaction as a developmental scaffold that tunes mentalizing networks and executive perspective-taking. The goal is not to claim the components are new, but to identify a sharper integrative claim.

```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "early childhood theory of mind develops through language-mediated social interaction that tunes mentalizing representations and executive self-other perspective control", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```

[result]: Recent work commonly links theory of mind to false-belief reasoning, mental-state language, caregiver mind-mindedness, executive function, and social experience, but these are often studied in partially separate literatures. The integrative mechanism appears moderately novel if framed as a developmental coupling process: conversational language and reciprocal interaction provide the training signal, while maturing mentalizing and prefrontal-control systems provide the representational and regulatory machinery. A promising contribution would specify developmental timing and task dissociations, such as early implicit sensitivity versus later explicit verbal false-belief success.
I’ll now propose a research synthesis that answers the academic question by combining the extracted mentalizing framework with executive-control mechanisms and the language/social-interaction literature. The proposed “gene genome” specifies the mechanism, niche, observations, limitations, deltas, and claim.

```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "gene_genome": {
    "mechanism_genome": "Theory of mind across early childhood is supported by a coupled developmental system: (1) social-brain/mentalizing mechanisms for representing agents, goals, emotions, desires, knowledge, and beliefs; (2) language mechanisms that encode and communicate mental states through vocabulary, complement syntax, narratives, and conversational pragmatics; and (3) prefrontal executive-control mechanisms that allow children to inhibit their own perspective, hold another person's perspective in working memory, and flexibly select the relevant belief or intention. Neural candidates include the temporoparietal junction, medial prefrontal cortex, posterior superior temporal sulcus, precuneus/posterior cingulate, and broader default-mode/social-cognition networks, with lateral prefrontal systems contributing controlled perspective-taking and task performance. Behaviorally, development proceeds from early sensitivity to gaze, emotion, goals, and joint attention; to toddler pretend play and desire reasoning; to preschool explicit false-belief and appearance-reality reasoning; and later to more complex recursive and second-order mental-state reasoning.",
    "niche_genome": "The synthesis targets normative early-childhood theory-of-mind development, especially ages 2 to 6, where children transition from social responsiveness and desire-based explanations to explicit belief reasoning. It is especially relevant for comparing children with different language environments, including variation in caregiver mental-state talk, conversational turn-taking, book reading, sibling and peer interaction, bilingual or multilingual exposure, and delayed access to a fully accessible language such as in some deaf children born to hearing parents. The framework also applies to individual differences associated with executive function, temperament, social opportunity, and neurodevelopmental variation.",
    "observation_genome": "Key observations are that explicit false-belief success typically improves around the preschool years, but younger children can show earlier partial sensitivity to others' goals, desires, ignorance, or perceptual access. Children's ToM performance is predicted by mental-state vocabulary, exposure to sentential-complement syntax such as 'she thinks that...', caregiver mind-mindedness, elaborative reminiscing, storybook conversations, pretend play, sibling interaction, and peer negotiation. Executive-function measures such as inhibition, working memory, and cognitive flexibility correlate with performance on standard ToM tasks, suggesting that some failures reflect difficulty managing competing perspectives rather than absence of all mental-state understanding. Neural evidence in older children and adults implicates mPFC, TPJ, pSTS, and precuneus/posterior cingulate in mentalizing, with developmental changes likely involving specialization and connectivity among these systems plus increasing recruitment of control networks for explicit reasoning.",
    "limitation_genome": "The evidence base has several limitations: infant and toddler implicit ToM findings remain debated; many preschool tasks confound mental-state reasoning with language comprehension, memory, and inhibition; neural data in very young children are sparse because fMRI and task compliance are difficult; and much of the literature is culturally and socioeconomically narrow. Language exposure is also multidimensional, making it difficult to separate vocabulary, syntax, conversational pragmatics, narrative experience, and social access. Correlational links between caregiver talk, executive function, and ToM do not by themselves establish causal direction, because socially skilled children may elicit richer mental-state talk and more complex interaction.",
    "delta_genome": "The proposed advance is to treat language exposure and social interaction not as external predictors of ToM, but as the main developmental training regime for mentalizing. Conversational exchanges force children to track who knows what, why speakers say what they say, how perspectives differ, and how beliefs can conflict with reality; language supplies symbolic formats for representing hidden mental states; and social interaction supplies live error correction through misunderstanding, deception, teasing, pretend play, repair, and explanation. This reframes executive function as a gatekeeper of explicit ToM expression and language as both representational medium and social input stream, rather than as merely a covariate.",
    "claim_genome": "Across early childhood, theory of mind develops through reciprocal coupling between maturing neural systems for social representation, language-mediated conceptual learning, and prefrontal control of perspective conflict. Rich and accessible language exposure, especially mental-state discourse and syntactically complex conversation, accelerates children's ability to form explicit belief representations, while frequent reciprocal social interaction gives those representations pragmatic function. Thus, ToM development is best explained not by a single maturation event or a purely linguistic account, but by an interactionist mechanism in which social experience teaches children what minds are for, language teaches them how minds can be represented, and executive-control development enables them to deploy those representations in demanding tasks."
  }
}
```

[result]: Proposed synthesis: early childhood theory of mind is a language-mediated, socially trained, neurally distributed capacity rather than a single cognitive module. The central developmental pathway links social interaction and mental-state discourse to increasingly explicit belief reasoning, with mPFC/TPJ-centered mentalizing systems providing representational structure and prefrontal control supporting self-other perspective management. This directly addresses the prompt by explaining both neural/behavioral mechanisms and how language exposure and social interaction shape their developmental expression.

topic: [cognitive_science/academic_question] How does chronic exposure to digital multitasking environments affect attentional control, metacognitive monitoring, and long-term learning outcomes in adolesce
full_prompt: How does chronic exposure to digital multitasking environments affect attentional control, metacognitive monitoring, and long-term learning outcomes in adolescents and adults?
completion:
I’ll search once to anchor the proposal in recent cognitive-science evidence, then move directly to a mechanistic research proposal spanning adolescents and adults.
Rationale: The topic is broad but clear: chronic digital multitasking should be framed not merely as “screen time,” but as repeated exposure to interruption, task-switching, reward cues, and external cognitive offloading. I’ll search once for recent work connecting digital environments, attention, learning, and metacognitive consequences.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"digital multitasking chronic exposure attentional control metacognition learning adolescents adults screen time cognitive offloading 2018 2025","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```

[result]: The search surfaced recent work on cognitive costs of AI-era convenience and offloading, developmental concerns around excessive screen exposure, stress and experience sampling in digital contexts, digital well-being and algorithmic burnout, and evidence on study strategies and academic achievement. The strongest directly relevant anchors are oa:W4417024233 on cognition outsourcing, oa:W4210792459 on excessive screen exposure during development, and oa:W4256161118 on learning strategies and achievement. Together, these suggest a gap: chronic digital multitasking is often studied through screen time or media use, but less often as an experimentally and longitudinally measurable ecology of interruptions, switching, metacognitive miscalibration, and durable learning outcomes.

Rationale: I’ll now propose a concise, testable research program that separates mere digital exposure from switch-contingent attentional training and metacognitive monitoring failure.

```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Chronic digital multitasking environments repeatedly pair salient cues, partial task completion, rapid context switching, and external cognitive offloading. Over time, this may bias attentional control toward reactive capture rather than proactive goal maintenance, increase switch-cost normalization, and weaken metacognitive monitoring by making fluency, availability, and completion cues feel like genuine understanding. The proposed mechanism is a three-part cascade: interruption-conditioned attentional instability → degraded monitoring of comprehension and memory strength → poorer delayed retention and transfer despite preserved or inflated subjective confidence.","niche_genome":"Adolescents and adults who inhabit high-frequency digital multitasking environments, including notification-rich smartphone use, simultaneous study/work and media use, social media checking during academic tasks, and AI/search-based cognitive offloading. Adolescents are expected to show stronger effects because attentional-control and metacognitive systems are still developing, while adults may show more strategy-dependent effects moderated by occupational demands, baseline executive control, and self-regulation habits.","observation_genome":"Empirically, heavy digital multitaskers should show larger task-switching costs, poorer sustained attention, greater susceptibility to distractor interference, and more frequent mind-wandering during learning. Metacognitively, they should show poorer calibration between confidence or judgments of learning and later test performance, especially after interrupted study episodes. Long-term learning outcomes should be most impaired on delayed recall, conceptual transfer, source memory, and integration across materials, rather than on immediate recognition or shallow factual quizzes.","limitation_genome":"Existing studies often conflate total screen time with multitasking structure, rely on self-report media-use measures, use cross-sectional designs, or measure immediate performance rather than delayed learning. Many also undermeasure metacognition, treating attention and academic performance as separate outcomes. A further limitation is causal ambiguity: individuals with weaker attentional control may self-select into multitasking environments, rather than being impaired by them.","delta_genome":"The proposed study would combine passive digital trace data, ecological momentary assessment, and laboratory learning tasks across adolescents and adults. It would quantify not only screen duration but interruption density, app-switch rate, notification responsiveness, concurrent media use, and offloading frequency. Participants would complete attention-control tasks, confidence-calibrated learning tasks, judgments of learning, delayed retention tests, and transfer tests across repeated waves. A micro-randomized intervention could temporarily reduce notifications or impose single-task study blocks to test whether reducing switch density improves metacognitive calibration and delayed learning.","claim_genome":"The central claim is that chronic digital multitasking harms learning less by consuming time alone and more by training a maladaptive attentional-metacognitive profile: learners become more interruptible, less accurate in judging what they know, and more dependent on fluency and external access cues. Adolescents should be especially vulnerable to this profile, while adults may be protected by stronger metacognitive strategies and deliberate single-task routines. The strongest prediction is an exposure-by-delay interaction: high multitasking exposure will predict relatively modest deficits on immediate performance but larger deficits in calibrated confidence, delayed retention, and transfer."}}
```

[result]: Proposed research trajectory: Treat digital multitasking as a measurable cognitive ecology rather than a generic screen-time variable. The key hypothesis is that chronic interruption and switching degrade proactive attentional control and metacognitive calibration, producing delayed learning costs that are larger than immediate-performance costs, especially in adolescents.

The São Paulo metropolitan freight measures paper is directly relevant because it examines how local authorities respond to livability complaints from freight traffic while balancing logistics needs.

The São Paulo paper appears close enough to read once for policy mechanisms and limitations before proposing a transferable intervention.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W4291564881"}
```

Given the evidence, I’ll propose an integrated “freight impact district” approach: route hierarchy, managed curbs/loading, clean-truck requirements, and land-use performance standards rather than isolated truck bans.

[result]: Proposed recovery strategy: search by transport mode synonyms and mapping methods before adding equity terminology. The strongest methodological pathway is informal-network reconstruction plus accessibility analysis, rather than searching only for “informal transit equity.”

topic: [cognitive_science/academic_question] To what extent do embodied cognition processes contribute to abstract concept learning, particularly in domains such as mathematics, morality, or time perceptio
full_prompt: To what extent do embodied cognition processes contribute to abstract concept learning, particularly in domains such as mathematics, morality, or time perception?
completion:
I first map the recent literature around embodied abstract concepts, because the prompt spans several domains—mathematics, morality, and time perception—and likely requires distinguishing “grounding” from stronger claims that bodily systems are necessary for learning.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"embodied cognition abstract concept learning mathematics morality time perception gesture sensorimotor grounding","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```  
[result]: The search foregrounds work on grounded/embodied semantics, gesture and mathematical reasoning, spatial–temporal metaphors, and affective/interoceptive grounding of moral concepts. It also shows a split between strong embodiment accounts, which treat sensorimotor systems as constitutive of concept learning, and pluralist accounts, which allow linguistic, social, and distributional mechanisms to support abstraction. Several papers emphasize that evidence is strongest for online processing effects—gesture, spatial mapping, action simulation—but weaker for necessity claims about abstract concept acquisition.

Because abstract concepts are often culturally learned, I next read a broader paper on cognition and culture to see whether embodied processes are treated as individual sensorimotor mechanisms or as socially stabilized practices.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W2947609478"}
```  
[result]: The paper “Thinking through other minds: A variational approach to cognition and culture” frames cognition and cultural learning in terms of predictive/variational processes distributed across individuals and shared practices. Its relevance is that abstract norms, expectations, and meanings are learned not only by internal representation but by participation in culturally scaffolded action routines. For the present question, it suggests that embodiment should include socially coordinated perception–action loops—gesture, ritual, posture, interaction—not merely neural motor simulation.

I then narrow the search to the three target domains so the proposal can specify where embodied contributions are likely strongest versus weaker.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"2018 2025 embodied cognition mathematics learning gesture morality time perception abstract concepts review","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```  
[result]: Results cluster into three streams: gesture/manipulatives in mathematics education; embodied metaphors and moral cognition, including purity, disgust, verticality, and social distance; and spatial/action-based accounts of time perception. Mathematics studies often report that gesture and concrete action improve early learning or transfer when mapped carefully onto formal structure. Morality and time perception studies more often show embodied modulation of judgments rather than full concept acquisition, suggesting domain-specific strength of evidence.

To avoid proposing a claim that merely restates “abstract concepts are embodied,” I check whether a more precise mechanism—graded, scaffolded embodiment varying by abstraction level and cultural conventionalization—appears novel enough.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","mechanism":"Embodied processes contribute to abstract concept learning through graded scaffold alignment: sensorimotor, affective, and interoceptive routines initially anchor abstract concepts, but linguistic and cultural practices progressively compress these routines into portable symbolic schemas; the strength of embodiment varies by domain and learning stage.","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025}
```  
[result]: The mechanism overlaps with grounded cognition, conceptual metaphor theory, and pluralist/distributed accounts of abstract concepts, so it is not wholly novel. However, the specific graded claim—stronger embodiment during acquisition and instructional scaffolding, weaker and more optional during expert symbolic use—appears to be a useful synthesis. The novelty lies in comparing mathematics, morality, and time perception using the same developmental and mechanistic continuum rather than treating embodiment as a single yes/no property.

The final step is to formulate a researchable answer: embodied cognition contributes substantially, but its contribution is conditional on domain, developmental stage, and the availability of linguistic-cultural scaffolds.  
```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Abstract concept learning is supported by graded embodied scaffolding: learners recruit sensorimotor action, gesture, spatial mapping, affective/interoceptive states, and socially coordinated routines to stabilize initially abstract relations. With practice, these embodied routines are partially compressed into linguistic, symbolic, and cultural schemas, so expert reasoning can appear disembodied while still retaining traces of bodily grounding.","niche_genome":"Best suited for cognitive science questions about abstract domains where concepts are not directly perceptual but are learned through action, interaction, metaphor, and instruction—especially early mathematics, moral evaluation, and time perception. The framework targets developmental, educational, and cross-cultural contexts from 2018–2025 debates on grounded cognition, predictive processing, conceptual metaphor, and cultural learning.","observation_genome":"In mathematics, gesture, finger use, spatial layout, manipulatives, and diagrammatic action often support acquisition of number, magnitude, algebraic relations, and proof-relevant structure, particularly when bodily actions map transparently onto formal relations. In morality, bodily affect and interoception—such as disgust, purity, harm sensitivity, posture, or social distance—modulate moral judgments, but moral learning also depends heavily on language, norms, and institutions. In time perception, spatialized bodily experience—movement, directionality, rhythm, and cultural writing/reading direction—shapes temporal metaphors and judgments, though mature temporal reasoning can be maintained symbolically.","limitation_genome":"Embodiment evidence is strongest for facilitation, framing, and online processing, but weaker for claims that bodily simulation is strictly necessary for all abstract concept learning. Many findings risk overgeneralization because effects vary by task, culture, age, expertise, and linguistic context. Embodied cues can also mislead learners when surface action conflicts with formal structure, as in poorly designed manipulatives or metaphors.","delta_genome":"Move from a binary question—are abstract concepts embodied or amodal?—to a graded developmental model asking when, how, and for whom embodied processes matter. The key delta is to compare domains by their dominant grounding channel: mathematics relies strongly on sensorimotor-spatial action during early learning; morality relies on affective, interoceptive, and social embodiment; time perception relies on spatial, rhythmic, and action-based mappings. The model predicts decreasing dependence on overt embodiment as symbolic fluency and cultural conventionalization increase, but persistent reactivation under difficult, novel, or pedagogical conditions.","claim_genome":"Embodied cognition contributes meaningfully but not exhaustively to abstract concept learning. Its contribution is strongest as an acquisition and scaffolding mechanism, moderate as a source of metaphorical and affective constraint, and weakest as a necessary substrate for expert abstract reasoning. Therefore, mathematics, morality, and time perception are best explained by a hybrid account in which bodily experience, language, social practice, and symbolic systems jointly construct abstract concepts."}}
```  
[result]: Proposed answer: embodied processes are important to abstract concept learning to the extent that they provide early anchors, attentional structure, affective salience, and culturally shared action routines. They should not be treated as the sole basis of abstraction, because language, pedagogy, social norms, and symbolic notation can transform and sometimes replace overt bodily grounding. A defensible academic conclusion is thus “graded embodiment”: strong in learning and transfer when bodily structure aligns with conceptual structure; weaker, indirect, or optional in fluent symbolic reasoning.

topic: [urban_planning/industry_problem] We need to prioritize climate resilience investments across neighborhoods facing flooding, heat, and aging infrastructure, but our capital budget is limited. Ho
full_prompt: We need to prioritize climate resilience investments across neighborhoods facing flooding, heat, and aging infrastructure, but our capital budget is limited. How can we build an equity-focused decision framework that ranks projects by risk reduction, community benefit, and implementation feasibility?
completion:
I’ll ground the framework in recent urban resilience and adaptation-planning literature, focusing on equity, multi-hazard risk, and capital-program prioritization. One search is sufficient because the problem is applied and the decision framework can be proposed directly.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "urban climate resilience equity project prioritization flooding heat infrastructure multi criteria decision framework", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```

[result]: The search surfaces recent work on equity-centered urban resilience, climate adaptation prioritization, infrastructure vulnerability, and multi-criteria decision analysis. Relevant candidates include Meerow et al. on social equity in urban resilience planning, studies on heat/flood vulnerability indices, and applied MCDA approaches for local climate adaptation investment decisions. The literature converges on the need to combine physical exposure, social vulnerability, co-benefits, implementation feasibility, and procedural justice rather than ranking projects by avoided damages alone.

Given the applied nature of the prompt, I’ll propose a practical decision framework: a transparent, equity-weighted capital prioritization model that cities can use across neighborhoods and project types.

The framework ranks projects by risk reduction, equity need, community benefit, feasibility, and readiness while including safeguards against underinvestment bias and displacement.

topic: [cognitive_science/industry_problem] As a product manager for an educational app, how can we personalize practice schedules using cognitive science principles like spacing and retrieval to improve
full_prompt: As a product manager for an educational app, how can we personalize practice schedules using cognitive science principles like spacing and retrieval to improve long-term retention?
completion:
Spacing + retrieval practice are mature enough to translate directly into product logic; the key industry question is how to operationalize them without overburdening learners. I’ll do one targeted evidence scan, then propose a product mechanism.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"spacing effect retrieval practice adaptive scheduling educational app long term retention cognitive science","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```

[result]: Recent cognitive-science and learning-engineering work supports combining spaced repetition with active retrieval, especially when schedules adapt to item difficulty, learner success, and elapsed time. Evidence also suggests that simple “next best review” algorithms can outperform fixed review calendars, but products must manage motivation, workload, and curriculum constraints. The strongest practical pattern is to schedule reviews just before predicted forgetting, using quiz performance and confidence as signals.

Given that evidence, I’ll propose a product-ready personalization system: an adaptive practice scheduler that estimates memory strength per learner-skill-item and assigns short retrieval sessions at optimal intervals.

```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Build an adaptive spacing-and-retrieval scheduler for the educational app. For each learner, maintain a memory-strength estimate for every skill, concept, or question family. Update the estimate after each retrieval attempt using correctness, response time, hint use, confidence rating, and recency. Schedule the next practice when predicted recall probability falls into a target band, e.g. 70–85%, so practice is effortful but not discouraging. Use active recall formats first—short answer, flashcard, problem solving, explanation—before showing notes or worked examples.","niche_genome":"Best suited for educational apps with repeatable knowledge or procedural skills: language vocabulary, math facts, coding syntax, science concepts, test prep, medical or professional training, and compliance learning. The product niche is learners who need durable retention across weeks or months, not just same-day completion. It can be used in consumer learning apps, school homework tools, corporate learning platforms, and exam-prep products.","observation_genome":"Learners often complete practice in bursts, perform well immediately, and then forget before the next unit, exam, or real-world use. Static review schedules ignore individual differences: one learner may need another review tomorrow, while another can wait ten days. Cognitive science suggests that durable retention improves when learners repeatedly retrieve information after increasing delays, especially when retrieval is effortful and feedback is immediate.","limitation_genome":"The scheduler must avoid becoming a black-box source of endless review tasks. If too aggressive, it can crowd out new learning and reduce motivation. If too lenient, learners forget before practice occurs. The system also needs cold-start logic for new learners and safeguards for curriculum deadlines, teacher assignments, and high-stakes exam dates. Prediction accuracy may be weaker for open-ended skills than for discrete facts or problem types.","delta_genome":"Compared with a generic spaced-repetition feature, the proposed system personalizes at the learner-skill level and blends cognitive-science signals with product constraints. It does not simply repeat missed questions; it maps questions to underlying skills, schedules varied retrieval prompts, and adapts intervals based on demonstrated recall strength. The product delta is a ‘retention engine’ that chooses what to review, when to review it, and in what format, while balancing new content, review load, learner goals, and deadlines.","claim_genome":"An educational app can improve long-term retention by replacing fixed practice calendars with an adaptive retrieval scheduler that targets the moment just before likely forgetting. The core PM recommendation is to ship a daily personalized practice queue: 5–15 minutes of mixed, effortful retrieval selected by memory-strength estimates, followed by immediate feedback and interval adjustment. Success should be measured not only by daily engagement or lesson completion, but by delayed retention: accuracy after 7, 14, and 30 days, reduced relearning time, and transfer to cumulative assessments."}}
```

[result]: Proposed product mechanism: an adaptive “retention engine” that personalizes practice schedules using spacing, retrieval difficulty, and learner-specific forgetting estimates. This is suitable for an educational app PM because it translates cognitive science into concrete roadmap components, ranking logic, learner experience, and retention metrics.

topic: [urban_planning/conference_cfp] This session calls for research on post-pandemic transformations in public space, downtown revitalization, and mixed-use urban districts. Papers may investigate
full_prompt: This session calls for research on post-pandemic transformations in public space, downtown revitalization, and mixed-use urban districts. Papers may investigate changing work patterns, street redesign, commercial recovery strategies, or the long-term planning implications of hybrid urban life.
completion:
I’ll start by locating work that directly bridges post-pandemic public-space change, downtown recovery, and mixed-use districts, then use the provided OpenAlex candidates as anchors. The goal is to identify a CFP-ready gap rather than simply summarize pandemic urbanism.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"post-pandemic downtown revitalization public space hybrid work mixed-use districts urban planning","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```  
[result]: The search surfaced a small cluster around “post-pandemic city” health/wellbeing, hybrid-work impacts on central business districts, adaptive reuse, and street/public-realm redesign. The two strongest anchor records from the provided set are `oa:W4385700142` on health and wellbeing in the post-pandemic city and `oa:W4387267213` on adaptive reuse and workforce revitalization. The results suggest the literature is fragmented: public health, real estate reuse, and downtown economic recovery are often treated separately.

Next I’ll read the broader post-pandemic city paper to see how it frames public space, urban life, and planning implications after COVID-19. This should help establish the conceptual baseline for a conference-style contribution.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W4385700142"}
```  
[result]: `Health and Wellbeing in the Post-Pandemic City` frames the pandemic as a stress test for urban environments, emphasizing health, public space access, social infrastructure, and governance capacity. It highlights how lockdowns, distancing, and uneven exposure reshaped everyday urban experience, but its planning implications are relatively broad rather than focused on downtown commercial recovery. The paper is useful for grounding the project in post-pandemic urban wellbeing, but it leaves room for a more district-scale account of hybrid work and revitalization.

I’ll now read the adaptive reuse paper because reuse of obsolete office, industrial, and commercial space is central to downtown recovery debates. I’m looking for mechanisms connecting built-form transformation to labor markets and mixed-use vitality.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W4387267213"}
```  
[result]: `Adaptive reuse and its impact on workforce revitalization` treats adaptive reuse as an economic development strategy, especially in post-industrial contexts where historic buildings can support new employment ecosystems. It connects reuse to workforce revitalization, place identity, and local development, but its primary emphasis is not post-pandemic hybrid work or the temporal rhythms of downtown public space. The paper provides a useful redevelopment mechanism, yet it does not fully address how changing daily work patterns alter demand for streets, retail, transit, and civic programming.

Before proposing a paper, I’ll test whether the specific mechanism I’m considering—hybrid-work temporal redistribution plus adaptive reuse and public-realm programming—is already saturated. This helps sharpen the contribution beyond generic “post-pandemic downtown recovery.”  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","mechanism":"Hybrid work redistributes downtown activity across days and times, requiring coordinated adaptive reuse, public-space programming, and mixed-use district governance to revitalize post-pandemic downtowns.","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025}
```  
[result]: The novelty check finds substantial work on hybrid work and office demand, plus separate literatures on tactical urbanism, adaptive reuse, and downtown economic recovery. However, fewer studies explicitly treat temporal redistribution—midweek peaks, Monday/Friday troughs, evening/weekend substitution—as a planning mechanism linking public space, retail recovery, and reuse policy. The mechanism appears promising if framed as a district-scale governance and design problem rather than as a real estate vacancy issue alone.

I’ll now convert the gap into a conference CFP-style research proposal. The proposal will position hybrid urban life as a temporal and spatial planning challenge for mixed-use downtown districts.  
```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Post-pandemic hybrid work has desynchronized downtown activity: fewer predictable five-day office routines reduce weekday lunch-hour retail demand and transit peaks, while residential, leisure, civic, and evening/weekend uses become more important to district vitality. Downtown revitalization therefore depends on coordinating three mechanisms—adaptive reuse of underused buildings, public-space redesign/programming, and mixed-use governance that manages activity across different times of day and week.","niche_genome":"Urban planning research on post-pandemic transformations in public space and downtown mixed-use districts, with emphasis on central business districts and post-industrial downtowns facing office vacancy, retail fragility, and changing street life between 2018 and 2025.","observation_genome":"Existing post-pandemic city scholarship foregrounds health, wellbeing, and access to public space, while adaptive reuse scholarship shows how building conversion can support workforce and economic revitalization. Yet many downtown recovery strategies still assume that restoring pre-pandemic office foot traffic is the main path to recovery. Early evidence from hybrid work suggests a different pattern: downtowns are not simply empty, but temporally uneven, with activity shifting toward selected midweek days, special events, residential-serving uses, and evening/weekend programming.","limitation_genome":"Current studies often treat public-space redesign, commercial recovery, hybrid work, and adaptive reuse as separate planning domains. This separation obscures how street design, storefront survival, office conversion, transit scheduling, and civic programming interact at the district scale. It also limits policy guidance because downtown recovery is measured through aggregate vacancy or footfall rather than through temporal mixes of users, uses, and public-space intensities.","delta_genome":"The proposed paper advances a temporal mixed-use framework for post-pandemic downtown revitalization. The analysis would identify which combinations of reuse policy, street redesign, and programming help districts convert hybrid-work disruption into more resilient all-day, all-week urban life.","claim_genome":"Conference paper title: “From Office Peak to Urban Rhythm: Hybrid Work, Public Space, and the Temporal Revitalization of Downtown Mixed-Use Districts.” The paper argues that post-pandemic downtown recovery should not be evaluated by whether central business districts return to pre-2020 commuter routines. Instead, successful revitalization will depend on planning for new urban rhythms: converting obsolete workspaces, diversifying ground-floor economies, redesigning streets as civic and social infrastructure, and governing downtowns as mixed-use districts whose vitality is distributed across multiple publics and temporalities."}}
```

